result.chapter_summary: Hirsch defines communicative efficiency as 'relative readability,' arguing that it is not a utilitarian constraint on aesthetics but a principle relative to a writer's specific semantic intentions. He distinguishes prose efficiency, which is reader-centered, from oral efficiency, which balances speaker and listener effort, and identifies Herbert Spencer's concept of 'mental economy' as the foundational principle of this theory.

claims.text: Communicative efficiency in prose is frequently and gravely misunderstood as being opposed to aesthetic and expressive values.
claims.source_passage: Efficiency is a utilitarian goal that is apparently opposed to the aesthetic and expressive values of literature. If I now pause to explain again in greater detail just why my use of the words efficiency and readability are unopposed to aesthetic and other ‘nonutilitarian’ values, I must ask the forbearance of those readers who have already grasped my point.

claims.text: Ideological passions and confrontations arise whenever a specific goal is named for the teaching of composition.
claims.source_passage: Ideological passions arise whenever one names any goal for the teaching of composition.

claims.text: The traditional goal of 'clarity' in composition is often criticized as enforcing conformity to lifeless norms at the expense of individuality.
claims.source_passage: Probably my word efficiency is even less attractive than the word clarity, which implies to Lanham a conformity to lifeless norms at the expense of individuality.

claims.text: Communicative efficiency means the most efficient communication of any semantic intention, regardless of whether that intention is conformist or individualistic.
claims.source_passage: But no such charge can properly be aimed at the goal of communicative efficiency, which means simply the most efficient communication of any semantic intention, whether it be conformist or individualistic.

claims.text: Some semantic intentions require prose that is complex and difficult to read; in such cases, simple prose would be inefficient.
claims.source_passage: Some semantic intentions require prose that is complex and difficult to read. An attempt to express those intentions in easy-to-read prose would properly be condemned as inefficient writing.

claims.text: Communicative efficiency is synonymous with relative readability.
claims.source_passage: Communicative efficiency is synonymous with relative readability, and both imply the relativity of my criterion to the writer’s semantic intentions.

claims.text: Efficiency in oral speech and efficiency in written prose are governed by different sets of requirements.
claims.source_passage: Another potential misconception of efficiency can arise when the distinction between linguistic efficiency in oral speech and linguistic efficiency in prose is forgotten.

claims.text: Efficiency in oral speech requires a balance between the effort of the speaker and the effort of the listener.
claims.source_passage: The theory of progressive efficiency in language evolution was applied by Jespersen, Zipf, and Martinet to oral speech, and they agreed that the appraisal of efficiency had to take account of both sides of oral speech—the speaker's side as well as the listener’s.

claims.text: The appraisal of efficiency in written prose is entirely one-sided, focused exclusively on the reader's ease of understanding.
claims.source_passage: By contrast, the appraisal of efficiency in prose is entirely one-sided—hence my equation of efficiency in prose with readability.

claims.text: Achieving efficiency for the reader often requires an increase in effort and labor for the writer.
claims.source_passage: For the reader, the longer way is often the faster way, and what is understood easily and rapidly by the reader may be the result of five or six painstaking revisions by the writer.

claims.text: Readability encompasses 'rhetorical efficiency,' which is the success of prose in affecting the reader in ways beyond the mere transmission of information.
claims.source_passage: Implicit in this conception of readability is the rhetorical efficiency of prose—its success in affecting the implied reader in ways that transcend the mere conveying of information.

claims.text: The goal of relative readability is fundamental to all speech and transcends personal preference or historical evolution.
claims.source_passage: For, the goal of readability in prose implies values that are fundamental to all of speech.

claims.text: A number of Victorian authors misguidedly resisted the historical evolution toward increased readability.
claims.source_passage: But this historical process can be resisted, and in fact was resisted by a number of authors in the Victorian age. Theirs was a misguided effort, as the majority of their Victorian contemporaries understood.

claims.text: Herbert Spencer's 1852 essay 'The Philosophy of Style' provides a foundational and still-unsuperseded account of the principles of readability.
claims.source_passage: This essay, though not widely noticed in recent work, has never been superseded. In some of his observations Spencer was almost certainly wrong, but he was rarely wrong in the general principles he enunciated.

claims.text: The phrases 'economy of the reader’s attention' and 'least possible mental effort' are the historical equivalents of the modern concept of readability.
claims.source_passage: Although Spencer did not himself use the word readability, he used the meaning, which he expressed in the phrases 'economy of the reader’s attention' and 'least possible mental effort.'

claims.text: Recent research in linguistics, psycholinguistics, and psychology has confirmed Spencer's intuitive hunches about language processing.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, some of his genial hunches have been confirmed by very recent research in linguistics, psycholinguistics, and psychology.

claims.text: All isolated dogmas and maxims of rhetoric and composition can be reduced to a single organizing principle.
claims.source_passage: Spencer announced his goal to be the reduction of all rhetorical maxims to a single principle. ‘The maxims contained in works on composition and rhetoric are presented in an unorganized form. Standing as isolated dogmas—as empirical generalizations, they are neither so clearly apprehended...'

claims.text: Herbert Spencer's essay 'The Philosophy of Style' has never been superseded in its general principles regarding readability.
claims.source_passage: This essay, though not widely noticed in recent work, has never been superseded. In some of his observations Spencer was almost certainly wrong, but he was rarely wrong in the general principles he enunciated.

claims.text: Rhetorical maxims in standard handbooks are isolated dogmas that should be deduced from a single first principle.
claims.source_passage: Standing as isolated dogmas—as empirical generalizations, they are neither so clearly apprehended, nor so much respected, as they would be were they deduced from some simple first principle.

claims.text: The law underlying current rhetorical maxims is the importance of economizing the reader’s or hearer’s attention.
claims.source_passage: On seeking for some clue to the law underlying these current maxims, we may see implied in many of them, the importance of economizing the reader’s or hearer’s attention.

claims.text: The standard of judgment for condemning writing as wordy or confused is the assumption that writing should require the least possible mental effort.
claims.source_passage: When we condemn writing that is wordy or confused, or intricate—when we praise this style as easy, and blame that as fatiguing, we consciously or unconsciously assume this desideratum as our standard of judgment.

claims.text: Language is primarily a 'vehicle of thought' and a means to an end rather than an end in itself.
claims.source_passage: Behind Spencer's principle of least effort lay an assumption about the use of language which he quite explicitly announced: language is the 'vehicle of thought,' a means to an end rather than an end in itself.

claims.text: The principle of least effort is by itself an insufficient principle for guiding composition.
claims.source_passage: Spencer shows, without directly admitting it, that the principle of least effort is by itself an insufficient principle for composition. Other principles must be added besides the most efficient presentation of 'thoughts.'

claims.text: Stylistic variety is necessary to achieve interest, force, and delight (the economy of mental sensibilities).
claims.source_passage: The second part moves from a consideration of least effort to a consideration of greatest effect, and from 'the economy of mental energies' to the 'economy of the mental sensibilities.' Here, in this second part, Spencer deals with the importance of stylistic variety for achieving interest, force, and delight.

claims.text: Spencer committed a logical error by subsuming efficiency of word order and variety of presentation under the single concept of 'economy.'
claims.source_passage: His use of the single word 'economy' to mean both efficiency of word order and variety of presentation was a lexical sleight-of-hand. These stylistic traits are not double aspects of a single principle; Spencer has really subsumed two principles under the single word economy.

claims.text: Effective composition requires making constant compromises between conflicting psychological principles.
claims.source_passage: this error in logic prevented him from stressing the need for constant compromises between several sometimes conflicting psychological principles of composition.

claims.text: Cognitive effort spent on deciphering the meaning of symbols is energy subtracted from the ability to consider the meaning itself.
claims.source_passage: the time and effort devoted to the decipherment of meaning would be subtracted from the energy left to consider the meaning itself. This perhaps obvious point has recently been confirmed in psychological experiments.

claims.text: A reader possesses a 'limited channel-capacity' for processing information at any given moment.
claims.source_passage: Spencer was, I believe, the first writer to suggest that a reader has, in current terms, a 'limited channel-capacity': A reader or listener has at each moment but a limited amount of mental power available.

claims.text: A prose style that requires a second perusal is never justified if it can be avoided.
claims.source_passage: a prose style requiring a second perusal is never justified if such a style can be avoided.

claims.text: Monotony of style increases the cognitive effort required by the reader.
claims.source_passage: Monotony of style does increase the reader’s effort.

claims.text: The cognitive power required to recognize symbols and combine images reduces the power available for understanding the expressed thought.
claims.source_passage: To recognize and interpret the symbols presented to him requires part of this power; to arrange and combine the images suggested by them requires a further part; and only that part which remains can be used for framing the thought expressed.

claims.text: The more time and attention a reader spends understanding a sentence structure, the less vividly the contained idea will be conceived.
claims.source_passage: Hence the more time and attention it takes to receive and understand each sentence, the less time and attention can be given to the contained idea; and the less vividly will that idea be conceived.

claims.text: The historical preference for Saxon words over Latin words in English is justified by the shorter length of Saxon words.
claims.source_passage: For instance, the asserted superiority in English of Saxon words over Latin words he deduced from the shortness of the Saxon: 'If it be an advantage to express an idea in the smallest number of words, then it must be an advantage to express it in the smallest number of syllables.'

claims.text: Shorter words are more efficient because they require less time for pure decipherment, leaving more energy for processing meaning.
claims.source_passage: in a given span of time, less time and effort would be required for pure decipherment, and hence more energy would be left to consider meaning.

claims.text: A shorter expression is not always easier for a reader to process than a longer one.
claims.source_passage: In fact, however, as Martinet has rightly argued, a shorter expression is not always easier for a reader.

claims.text: The primary reason for the low energy-cost of Saxon words is their familiarity and frequency of use rather than their length.
claims.source_passage: The most important reason for the low energy-cost of Saxon words is their greater familiarity through their greater frequency of use.

claims.text: Words learned in childhood are more organically connected to ideas than synonyms learned later in life, leading to easier comprehension.
claims.source_passage: The synonyms learned in after years never become so closely, so organically connected with the ideas signified as do these original words used in childhood. ... Increasing familiarity ... brings greater rapidity and ease of comprehension.

claims.text: Determining the psychic energy needed to interpret written discourse requires analyzing both syntagmatic and paradigmatic aspects of language.
claims.source_passage: In this double explanation, Spencer has foreshadowed one of the epochal distinctions of modern linguistics—Saussure’s distinction between the syntagmatic and the paradigmatic aspects of language use, a distinction which, in some form or other, is required to determine the actual psychic energy needed to interpret a stretch of written discourse.

claims.text: Paradigmatic relations, though invisible on the page, are more important than syntagmatic relations for readability.
claims.source_passage: But their paradigmatic relations... though far more important, are quite invisible on the page.

claims.text: The optimal sequence for economizing reader attention is one that leaves the reader in uncertainty for the shortest period of time.
claims.source_passage: The best sequence for economizing the reader’s attention is the sequence which leaves the reader in uncertainty for the shortest period of time

claims.text: Narrative events should be stated in a sequence that prevents the reader from having to go 'backwards and forwards' mentally to connect them.
claims.source_passage: As in a narrative, the events should be stated in such sequence that the mind may not have to go backwards and forwards in order to rightly connect them

claims.text: Sentences within a group should be arranged so they can be understood individually without requiring information from subsequent sentences.
claims.source_passage: as in a group of sentences, the arrangement should be such that each of them may be understood as it comes without waiting for subsequent ones

claims.text: Spencer's argument that English adjective placement is inherently superior to French is provincial and incorrect.
claims.source_passage: His discussion of the superiority of English adjective placement over French is at once provincial and incorrect.

claims.text: Adjective-noun and noun-adjective placements are identically economical within their respective native linguistic domains.
claims.source_passage: Both normal versions are quite identically economical in their native domains, for both stay within [normal usage].

claims.text: The sequence of words in a sentence should suggest the constituents of thought in the order most convenient for building it up.
claims.source_passage: in every sentence, the sequence of words should be that which suggests the constituents of thought in the order most convenient for building it up.

claims.text: Writers of a modern grapholect have less syntactic choice than Herbert Spencer's analysis implies.
claims.source_passage: At times Spencer seems to imply that writers of a modern grapholect have much more syntactic choice than they actually do.

claims.text: English and French adjective-noun arrangements are identically economical because both remain within the bounds of short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Both normal versions are quite identically economical in their native domains, for both stay within the bounds of short-term memory

claims.text: The single most important factor in the syntactic dimension of readability is the duration between a qualifying member and the member qualified.
claims.source_passage: The longer the time that elapses between the mention of any qualifying member and the member qualified, the longer must the mind be exerted in carrying forward the qualifying member ready for use.

claims.text: Simultaneous qualifications in a sentence increase mental power expenditure and decrease the communicative effect.
claims.source_passage: And the more numerous the qualifications to be simultaneously remembered and rightly applied, the greater will be the mental power expended, and the smaller the effect produced.

claims.text: Syntactic force is gained by arranging sentence members so that suspensions are few in number and short in duration.
claims.source_passage: Hence, other things equal, force will be gained by so arranging the members of a sentence that these suspensions shall at any moment be the fewest in number, and shall also be of the shortest duration.

claims.text: Milton's poetry in Paradise Lost achieves high readability despite intricacy because its syntactic-semantic suspensions are brief.
claims.source_passage: Unquestionably, the syntactic-semantic suspensions of these lines are kept wonderfully brief, considering the intricacy and richness of the relationships expressed

claims.text: Metrical language is more effective than prose because rhythmic regularity allows the mind to economize energy by anticipating required attention.
claims.source_passage: metrical language is more effective than prose because it enables us to do this [anticipate attention].

claims.text: The principle of expectation-fulfillment is a crucial element in readability.
claims.source_passage: the principle of expectation-fulfillment.

claims.text: The easiest style is not always the best because it can lead to reader fatigue or boredom.
claims.source_passage: the easiest style is not always the best if it is achieved at the cost of boredom or 'fatigue.'

claims.text: The principle of least effort in prose is inherently relative to the author's specific intentions.
claims.source_passage: In this qualification of his principle, Spencer implicitly acknowledges its inherent relativity to authorial intentions.

claims.text: Variety in sentence style is necessary to prevent reader boredom and fatigue.
claims.source_passage: Spencer ends his essay with a psychological explanation for the importance of variety in sentence style, his point being that the easiest style is not always the best if it is achieved at the cost of boredom or ‘fatigue.’

claims.text: Economy of reader effort and the avoidance of reader fatigue are distinct considerations that an author must balance.
claims.source_passage: For there might very well be instances in which economy of reader effort is deemed more important than the probability of reader fatigue.

claims.text: The principle of 'other things equal' implies that the easiest style is best only when comparing methods of achieving the same semantic intention.
claims.source_passage: Other things equal, the easiest style is the best. In achieving the same semantic intentions, the easiest style is the best.

claims.text: The term 'readability' is often viewed negatively by scholars because of its association with basic educational testing.
claims.source_passage: Although the word readability conveys very well the idea of communicative efficiency in prose, it is tainted for some scholars by its almost exclusive association with testing the reading- difficulty of texts for young schoolchildren.

claims.text: Simple readability formulas based on word-length and sentence-length are empirically valid for predicting text difficulty.
claims.source_passage: If it could be shown that a formula with two variables (say, word-length and sentence-length) will consistently predict the reading-difficulty of a text just as accurately as more cumbersome formulas, then the simpler formula will have proved its practical validity.

claims.text: Rudolf Flesch's 'The Art of Readable Writing' is more useful and better informed than most college writing textbooks.
claims.source_passage: In fact, Dr. Flesch’s book is a useful production which goes far beyond the expansion and defense of these formulas. It is probably as useful as many college textbooks and better informed than most.

claims.text: Standard readability formulas fail to address the central issues of learning to write readable prose.
claims.source_passage: But neither Dr. Flesch’s book nor any of the readability formulas touch upon some of the crucial and central issues in learning the craft of writing readable prose.

claims.text: Readability formulas cannot discriminate between passages that score the same but differ significantly in actual readability.
claims.source_passage: they remain uninformative for the writer and the teacher of writing because they cannot discriminate between two passages which score the same, yet differ substantially in readability.

claims.text: Existing readability formulas can mistakenly identify a clarified, revised text as being more difficult than the original.
claims.source_passage: What does seem to me rather telling is the mathematical pronouncement that the second version is slightly more difficult to read. I believe this result is false for reasons that are beyond the power of any formula to correct.

claims.text: Traditional readability formulas (like Flesch RE and Devereaux GP) yield statistically insignificant differences when comparing semantically similar prose versions.
claims.source_passage: When I applied existing readability formulas to these two versions, I got very similar results—just three points difference on the Flesch RE formula, and less than one grade difference on the Devereaux GP formula. . . Probably, these differences are statistically insignificant.

claims.text: Readability formulas are inherently incapable of factoring in semantic contrasts between passages.
claims.source_passage: I believe this result is false for reasons that are beyond the power of any formula to correct, since not even the complex formulas of Bormuth can factor in the semantic contrasts between semantically similar passages.

claims.text: Meaning is the essential factor in assessing the readability of prose, yet it is excluded from arithmetical formulas.
claims.source_passage: It is in the nature of such formulas that meaning—the one thing needful—cannot be directly taken into account in assessing the readability of prose.

claims.text: Subjective, semantically based judgments should be used to supplement the results of arithmetical readability formulas.
claims.source_passage: Even there, one might hope that subjective, semantically based judgments about the readability of texts might be used to supplement the results of the formulas.

claims.text: Integrating meaning into the concept of readability makes precise arithmetical formulation impossible.
claims.source_passage: By introducing meaning into the concept of readability, we give up the possibility of precise arithmetical formulation.

claims.text: Readability should be gauged in relation to a text's intended meaning rather than an absolute scale of difficulty.
claims.source_passage: It is reasonable for an adult reader to gauge the readability of a text in relation to its intended meaning, not in relation to its degree of difficulty on an absolute scale.

claims.text: Relative readability is defined as the principle that, given two texts conveying the same meaning, the more readable text requires less time and effort to understand.
claims.source_passage: Assuming that two texts convey the same meaning, the more readable text will take less time and effort to understand.

claims.text: Relative readability is a stable property across different types of readers, including slow and fast readers or neophytes and experts.
claims.source_passage: This definition, while relative to meaning, is not relative to individual readers, since it has now been established that a text which is more readable for a slow reader is also more readable for a fast one, and a text which is more readable for a neophyte in a subject is also more readable for an expert.

claims.text: The existence of synonymy—the ability of different texts to convey the same meaning—is a necessary foundation for the teaching of composition.
claims.source_passage: I would go still further and say that in principle a revision of a prose passage must be able to convey the same meaning as an original version, if the teaching of composition is to have any point at all.

claims.text: The meaning of a prose passage is not the simple mathematical sum of its individual words and sentences.
claims.source_passage: For what the passage means is not determined by adding up the meanings of individual words and sentences, as if the semantic force of a prose passage were the sum of individual units of meaning.

claims.text: During prose reading, the reader typically forgets the specific linguistic forms (lexical and grammatical) shortly after reading them.
claims.source_passage: In reading a prose passage longer than a few dozen words, the reader usually cannot even remember the earlier lexical and grammatical forms of the passage.

claims.text: Human memory primarily stores prose information as meaning rather than as linguistic form.
claims.source_passage: It has been proved experimentally that what he mainly remembers is stored as meaning, not as language; and it is meaning, not linguistic form, which provides him with a context for understanding the part of the discourse he is currently reading.

claims.text: Lyric poetry is an exception to the normal pattern of linguistic forgetfulness because it demands attention to formal structure.
claims.source_passage: A short lyric poem does not, of course, conform to this normal pattern of linguistic forgetfulness. Yet most forms of writing, particularly the commoner forms of prose, are not read with the slowness and attention to form appropriate to the reading of lyric poetry.

claims.text: Poets often treat their semantic intentions as conveyable by alternative verbal forms.
claims.source_passage: The poet himself, like the humblest writer of prose, realizes that his semantic intentions are conveyed equally well (or badly) by alternative verbal forms, and he sometimes fails to decide which version will convey his intention best.

claims.text: Most forms of writing, particularly common prose, are not read with the slowness and attention to form used in lyric poetry.
claims.source_passage: Yet most forms of writing, particularly the commoner forms of prose, are not read with the slowness and attention to form appropriate to the reading of lyric poetry.

claims.text: Poetry is less demanding on memory (mnemonic demands) than is commonly assumed.
claims.source_passage: And even poetry is less exacting in its mnemonic demands than is sometimes assumed.

claims.text: A writer's semantic intentions can be conveyed equally well (or badly) by alternative verbal forms.
claims.source_passage: The poet himself, like the humblest writer of prose, realizes that his semantic intentions are conveyed equally well (or badly) by alternative verbal forms

claims.text: Two different texts can carry exactly the same meaning.
claims.source_passage: In another book, I have taken into account all of the objections against the possibility that two different texts can carry exactly the same meaning.

claims.text: If it were impossible to revise a text to convey the same meaning more readably, the teaching of composition would be unnecessary.
claims.source_passage: For, unless it were possible for a revised text to convey the same meaning as the original in a more readable way, we would not need to teach a student how to write readable prose.

claims.text: The primary role of a composition teacher is to enable students to convey their meanings efficiently.
claims.source_passage: What the ideal teacher of composition does teach is a skill that enables his students to convey their meanings efficiently.

claims.text: Persuasiveness, clarity, and elegance are not universal qualities of readable prose because they are not universal semantic intentions.
claims.source_passage: Persuasiveness, clarity, elegance may be parts of the student’s semantic intentions, or they may not be. Such qualities are not universal qualities of readable prose, because they are not universal semantic intentions for all writers at all times.

claims.text: There is a disconnect between the clarity of a writer's thought and the readability of their prose.
claims.source_passage: Let me give a brief example of the disconnection between clarity of thought and readability of prose.

claims.text: The relative readability of a passage can be improved without altering its semantic clarity.
claims.source_passage: The clarity of thought in that passage could scarcely be improved upon... But the relative readability of the excerpt can be rather easily improved.

claims.text: Prose can be highly readable and easy to understand while the underlying thought is illogical or 'muddy.'
claims.source_passage: The contrary sort of example—where the writing is highly readable and clear, but the thought is muddy—is often to be found in the letter columns of British newspapers.

claims.text: The relative readability and clarity of prose are governed more by psychological principles than by logical principles.
claims.source_passage: These examples suggest that the relative readability of prose, and even its relative “clarity,” are governed more by psychological principles than by logical ones.

claims.text: The logic of writing is distinct from the logic of thought.
claims.source_passage: The logic of writing is not the same as the logic of thought, and the clarity of a piece of writing is not the same as the clarity of its thought.

claims.text: Prose can be highly readable and easily understood even when it contains logical fallacies or non sequiturs.
claims.source_passage: But while the writing is clear, the thought is not, for the passage states in effect the following illogical argument... But the very repetitions of these phrases help make the prose readable and clearly understood.

claims.text: Instruction in conceptual clarity in composition courses may divert attention from the psychological principles that actually govern readability.
claims.source_passage: Even if conceptual clarity could be taught in a composition course, such instruction might divert attention from the psychological principles that chiefly govern the readability of writing.

claims.text: Reading ease is a narrow criterion that fails as a universal normative principle for writing.
claims.source_passage: Reading ease, by itself, is a narrow criterion which fails as a universal normative principle for writing.

claims.text: The concept of 'reading ease' is an oversimplification and trivialization of the norm of relative readability.
claims.source_passage: It is an oversimplification, even a trivialization of the norm that I have advocated here.

claims.text: The phrase 'intrinsic effectiveness' can serve as a synonym for 'relative readability' to prevent it from being confused with trivial 'reading ease.'
claims.source_passage: To reduce the danger that a reader might entertain such a misunderstanding, I shall, in what follows, occasionally substitute the phrase “intrinsic effectiveness” for “relative readability.”

claims.text: The ideological confrontations regarding composition goals at the 1966 Dartmouth conference hindered the development of a sense of common purpose among the participants.
claims.source_passage: Recently, Professor Lanham was moved to write a book-length attack on the traditional goal of clarity, and similar ideological confrontations emerged in discussions of composition at the Dartmouth conference of 1966, hindering a sense of common purpose among the conferees.

claims.text: The theory of progressive efficiency in language evolution, as defined by Jespersen, Zipf, and Martinet, requires balancing the effort of the speaker against the effort of the listener.
claims.source_passage: The theory of progressive efficiency in language evolution was applied by Jespersen, Zipf, and Martinet to oral speech, and they agreed that the appraisal of efficiency had to take account of both sides of oral speech—the speaker's side as well as the listener’s.

claims.text: The statistical norm for Victorian prose is found in popular newspapers rather than in the works of high-literary authors like Carlyle and Pater.
claims.source_passage: The statistical norm for Victorian prose is found in popular newspapers and My Secret Life, not in Carlyle and Pater.

claims.text: Spencer's principle of the 'limited amount of mental power' only holds true if one assumes a sentence is processed in a single perusal.
claims.source_passage: This statement holds true only if we assume that a sentence is given but one perusal, since we could read the sentence, or some parts of it, twice or thrice, attending fully to the meaning after we have deciphered the sentence.

claims.text: Longer words are sometimes more efficient than shorter synonyms because they provide the reader's consciousness more time to process the quality being predicated.
claims.source_passage: Magnificent is grander than grand, because it 'allows the reader’s consciousness more time to dwell on the quality predicated.'

claims.text: The primary goal of refining readability formulas is to achieve predictive validity using the simplest possible mathematical variables.
claims.source_passage: The aim of refining these readability formulas was to achieve the greatest possible predictive validity with the simplest possible means.

claims.text: For the teaching of composition to be logically coherent, one must assume the possibility that a revised text can convey the exact same meaning as an original.
claims.source_passage: I would go still further and say that in principle a revision of a prose passage must be able to convey the same meaning as an original version, if the teaching of composition is to have any point at all.

claims.text: Human memory prioritizes 'memory for gist' over linguistic form, which explains why people often misquote poetry even when they remember its meaning.
claims.source_passage: Almost everyone who quotes poetry without checking the text misquotes it. ... it has been proved experimentally that what he mainly remembers is stored as meaning, not as language.

claims.text: A composition teacher should not act as a 'tyrant' who dictates what a student's semantic intentions ought to be.
claims.source_passage: The composition teacher is not by profession a tyrant who tells his students what they ought to mean, though he may consider such instruction a secondary pedagogical duty.

claims.text: Repetition of key phrases can enhance the readability of a passage even when those phrases facilitate a logical non sequitur.
claims.source_passage: This is a straightforward non sequitur, built on a semantic slippage from an 'ideologically neutral approach' to an 'ideologically neutral universe.' But the very repetitions of these phrases help make the prose readable and clearly understood.

evidence.description: The 1966 Dartmouth Conference on the teaching of English, cited as an example of ideological conflict preventing common purpose in composition theory.
evidence.source_reference: H. J. Muller, The Uses of English (1967)

evidence.description: Richard Lanham's book-length attack on the goal of 'clarity' in style.
evidence.source_reference: R. A. Lanham, Style: An Anti-Textbook (1974)

evidence.description: William Faulkner's prose is used as an example of writing that is complex and 'difficult' on an absolute scale but 'efficient' and 'readable' relative to its complex semantic intentions.
evidence.source_reference: William Faulkner (various works)

evidence.description: The work of linguists Jespersen, Zipf, and Martinet regarding the evolution of efficiency in oral speech.
evidence.source_reference: Jespersen, Zipf, and Martinet

evidence.description: Herbert Spencer's 1852 essay 'The Philosophy of Style' in the Westminster Review.
evidence.source_reference: Herbert Spencer, 'The Philosophy of Style' (1852)

evidence.description: Standard rhetorical handbooks by Whateley, Kaimes, and Blair illustrate that existing rules implicitly rely on the principle of economizing attention.
evidence.source_reference: Whateley, Kaimes, and Blair (cited by Spencer)

evidence.description: Footnote 4 cites psychological research showing that stylistic monotony increases the processing effort for the reader.
evidence.source_reference: D. E. Broadbent, Perception and Communication (Oxford, 1958), pp. 135-38.

evidence.description: Psychological experiments (unnamed but noted as 'recent') confirming that decipherment effort subtracts from comprehension energy.
evidence.source_reference: unspecified recent psychological experiments

evidence.description: André Martinet's functional view of language, which argues that shortness does not always equate to ease of use.
evidence.source_reference: Martinet, A Functional View of Language, p. 141

evidence.description: The observation that a child's vocabulary is predominantly Saxon and that these early words retain stronger cognitive associations.
The comparison of semantic processing times for 'cat' versus 'feline' based on paradigmatic domain familiarity.
The comparison between English 'black horse' and French 'un cheval noir' as examples of linguistic conventions being equally efficient to native speakers.
Analysis of lines from Paradise Lost involving a 'prowling wolf' and a 'thief' as examples of brief suspensions in complex syntax.
The psychological effect of rhythm and rhyme in verse allowing for 'pre-adjustment' of perception.
evidence.source_reference: Spencer, Herbert, 'The Philosophy of Style'

evidence.description: The critique of Alexander Pope's style as being 'fatiguing' due to its excessive ease.
Spencer's critique of Alexander Pope's poetry as being 'too easy' and therefore fatiguing.
Rudolf Flesch's Reading Ease formula based on syllables per word and words per sentence.
Bormuth's 1966 study finding that relationships between language variables and difficulty are often non-linear (curvilinear).
evidence.source_reference: Bormuth (1966)

evidence.description: Hirsch's comparison of a passage by Bormuth and his own revision, where formulas showed negligible or counter-intuitive difficulty differences despite stylistic improvements.
Bormuth's research showing that the relationship between reading difficulty and readability formula traits is inconstant.
evidence.source_reference: J. R. Bormuth, 'New Developments in Readability Research,' 1967

evidence.description: Author's test applying Flesch and Devereaux formulas to two versions of a text, showing no significant measured difference despite felt differences.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch's own analysis in text

evidence.description: Bormuth's findings that readability improvements are consistent across different skill levels of readers (fast vs slow, neophyte vs expert).
evidence.source_reference: J. R. Bormuth, 'Readability, a New Approach,' 1966

evidence.description: Fillenbaum's experimental results regarding 'Memory for Gist,' showing people remember meaning rather than specific language.
evidence.source_reference: S. Fillenbaum, 'Memory for Gist: Some Relevant Variables,' 1966

evidence.description: Sachs's research on recognition memory for syntactic and semantic aspects of connected discourse.
evidence.source_reference: J. S. Sachs, 'Recognition Memory for Syntactic and Semantic Aspects of Connected Discourse'

evidence.description: Research confirming 'memory for gist'—the cognitive tendency to remember semantic meaning while forgetting syntax.
evidence.source_reference: Fillenbaum (1966), Sachs (1967), Johnson-Laird (1970), Levelt & Kempen (1975), Brewer (1975)
evidence.source_passage: This important finding, reconfirmed by several researchers, is described in the following publications: S. Fillenbaum, “Memory for Gist: Some Relevant Variables,” Language and Speech 9 (1966): 217-27; J. S. Sachs, “Recognition Memory for Syntactic and Semantic Aspects of Connected Discourse,” Perception and Psychophysics 2 (1967): 437-42

evidence.description: Analysis of Shakespeare's textual stability, showing he tolerated semantic variations in stage language.
evidence.source_reference: E. A. J. Honigmann, The Stability of Shakespeare’s Text (1965)
evidence.source_passage: His central example is Shakespeare, who, as a writer for the theater, would have recognized the semantic irrelevance of certain variations in stage language which, once spoken, is heard no more.

evidence.description: A passage from Cohen and Nagel's logic textbook that is intellectually clear but stylistically unreadable.
evidence.source_reference: M. Cohen and E. Nagel, An Introduction to Logic and Scientific Method (1934)
evidence.source_passage: In the previous chapters we have seen that the validity of a demonstration depends not on the truth or falsity of the premises, but upon their form or structure.

evidence.description: A letter from the Times Literary Supplement (TLS) that is stylistically clear but logically fallacious.
evidence.source_reference: Times Literary Supplement (TLS)
evidence.source_passage: In advancing an ideologically neutral approach to women’s history, Dr. Harrison does a grave injustice to the work of the new feminist polemicists.

evidence.description: A passage from a 1975 TLS review regarding Victorian women's history that is highly readable despite containing a semantic slippage and a non sequitur.
evidence.source_reference: Times Literary Supplement, December 19, 1975, p. 1517
evidence.source_passage: In advancing an ideologically neutral approach to women’s history, Dr. Harrison does a grave injustice to the work of the new feminist polemicists. ... The fact that many ordinary women were anti-feminist does not mean they were living in an ideologically neutral universe.

concepts.author_definition: The most efficient communication of a specific semantic intention, where the difficulty of the prose is measured against the complexity of the meaning intended by the author.
concepts.common_alternative: Absolute readability, often measured by simple word or sentence counts regardless of meaning.
concepts.source_passage: Communicative efficiency is synonymous with relative readability, and both imply the relativity of my criterion to the writer’s semantic intentions.

concepts.author_definition: The success of prose in affecting the reader in ways that go beyond the simple transfer of information, such as emotional or aesthetic impact.
concepts.source_passage: Implicit in this conception of readability is the rhetorical efficiency of prose—its success in affecting the implied reader in ways that transcend the mere conveying of information.

concepts.author_definition: A principle derived from Herbert Spencer, defining prose quality as the minimization of the mental effort required for the reader to apprehend the writer's meaning.
concepts.source_passage: Although Spencer did not himself use the word readability, he used the meaning, which he expressed in the phrases ‘economy of the reader’s attention’ and ‘least possible mental effort.’

concepts.author_definition: The finite amount of mental power available to a reader at any single moment, which must be shared between interpreting symbols and framing the thoughts those symbols express.
The psychological effects of stylistic variety, including interest, force, and delight, which go beyond the simple efficient delivery of ideas.
The aspect of language use consisting of the temporal/linear sequence of words and sentences in a discourse.
The invisible associations and domains of familiarity to which a word belongs, affecting its semantic processing speed.
The mental effort required to interpret symbols, arrange suggested images, and frame the contained thought of a sentence.
The time and cognitive space that elapses between the mention of a qualifying member and the member it qualifies in a sentence.
A cognitive mechanism in reading where regular patterns (like rhythm in verse) allow the mind to anticipate and pre-adjust to upcoming stimuli, thereby saving mental energy.
The psychological result of a style that is too monotonous or consistently easy, leading to boredom that may negate the benefits of cognitive economy.
Mathematical tools (like Flesch RE) designed to predict text difficulty based on surface linguistic traits like sentence and word length.
The principle that different verbal and syntactical arrangements can convey the exact same semantic intention or 'gist' to a reader.
concepts.common_alternative: The stylistic belief that any change in wording necessarily changes the meaning of a text.
concepts.source_passage: The crux in this definition of relative readability is the assumption that two different texts can convey the same meaning.

concepts.author_definition: The normal psychological process in prose reading where the reader discards specific lexical and grammatical forms in favor of storing the underlying meaning.
concepts.source_passage: In reading a prose passage longer than a few dozen words, the reader usually cannot even remember the earlier lexical and grammatical forms of the passage.

concepts.author_definition: An illogical shift in the meaning of a term within an argument, used to maintain superficial readability while obscuring logical errors.
A narrow, oversimplified criterion for writing that focuses on simplicity without regard for intended meaning or universal normative principles.
concepts.common_alternative: Often associated with readability formulas like Flesch-Kincaid.
concepts.source_passage: Reading ease, by itself, is a narrow criterion which fails as a universal normative principle for writing. It is an oversimplification, even a trivialization of the norm that I have advocated here.

concepts.author_definition: A synonym for relative readability used to emphasize that readability is a universal and inherent standard for successful written communication.
concepts.source_passage: I shall, in what follows, occasionally substitute the phrase “intrinsic effectiveness” for “relative readability.” ... it will serve as a reminder that relative readability is an intrinsic and truly universal norm of writing.

concepts.author_definition: The psychological flow and structural clarity of a text that makes it readable, as distinct from the logical validity of the arguments being made.
concepts.source_passage: The logic of writing is not the same as the logic of thought, and the clarity of a piece of writing is not the same as the clarity of its thought.

concepts.author_definition: A theory of language evolution suggesting language moves toward greater efficiency in terms of effort spent by speakers and listeners.
The first part of Spencer's principle focusing on the least effort required for presenting thoughts.
The standard of effectiveness for readability formulas, measuring how consistently a formula predicts reading difficulty.
The mental process of anticipating rhythmic patterns in verse to economize attention.
The psychological finding that readers remember the meaning (semantic content) of a text rather than its specific verbal forms.

cases.name: Victorian Resistance to Readability
cases.description: A group of Victorian authors who deliberately resisted the historical trend toward clearer, more readable prose. Hirsch characterizes this as a 'misguided effort' that was recognized as such even by many of their contemporaries.
key_passages: But this historical process can be resisted, and in fact was resisted by a number of authors in the Victorian age. Theirs was a misguided effort, as the majority of their Victorian contemporaries understood.
contestable_aspects: The characterization of Victorian stylistic experimentation as 'misguided' and the claim that the majority of contemporaries viewed it as such.

cases.name: The Philosophy of Style (Spencer, 1852)
cases.description: An essay by Herbert Spencer that attempts to ground all rhetorical advice in a single psychological principle of 'least effort.' Hirsch uses this as the historical prototype for his own theory of relative readability.
key_passages: This essay of Spencer’s is, I believe, the best introduction to the subject of readability, and a good starting point for my discussion of the topic.
That is probably the gravest intellectual flaw in his brilliant essay, for this error in logic prevented him from stressing the need for constant compromises

cases.name: Saxon vs. Latinate English
cases.description: The historical and stylistic debate in English over whether Saxon-derived words are superior to Latin-derived words. The author argues that Saxon words are more readable not just because they are shorter, but because they are part of a familiar 'paradigmatic domain' established in childhood.
key_passages: The most important reason for the low energy-cost of Saxon words is their greater familiarity through their greater frequency of use.

A child’s vocabulary is almost wholly Saxon.... The synonyms learned in after years never become so closely, so organically connected with the ideas signified as do these original words used in childhood.

cases.name: English vs. French Adjective Placement
cases.description: A comparison used to critique Spencer's linguistic provincialism. Spencer argued 'black horse' was inherently more logical/efficient than 'un cheval noir,' but Hirsch counters that efficiency is relative to the conventions of the native grapholect.
key_passages: His discussion of the superiority of English adjective placement over French is at once provincial and incorrect.
Both normal versions are quite identically economical in their native domains

cases.name: Paradise Lost vs. Aeropagitica
cases.description: The author compares Milton's poetry to his prose, arguing that the poetry is more readable because it manages syntactic suspensions more efficiently, despite its complex semantic content.
key_passages: relationships more intricate and rich than occur at the beginning of Aeropagitica, yet much more easily understood.

cases.name: Hirsch's Revision of Bormuth
cases.description: Hirsch takes a passage from Professor Bormuth regarding language variables and linearity and revises it for better clarity. He then applies the Flesch Reading Ease and Devereaux Grade Placement formulas to both. The formulas fail to register the improvement, with one even suggesting the more readable revision was slightly more difficult (suitable for 6th grade vs 5th grade).
key_passages: When I applied existing readability formulas to these two versions, I got very similar results—just three points difference on the Flesch RE formula, and less than one grade difference on the Devereaux GP formula.

What does seem to me rather telling is the mathematical pronouncement that the second version is slightly more difficult to read. I believe this result is false for reasons that are beyond the power of any formula to correct

cases.name: Shakespeare's Stage Language
cases.description: Hirsch uses Shakespeare (via Honigmann) as a case where the author realized that the specific verbal form (stage language) was semantically flexible as long as the intention was conveyed, allowing for textual variations.
key_passages: Shakespeare, who, as a writer for the theater, would have recognized the semantic irrelevance of certain variations in stage language which, once spoken, is heard no more.

cases.name: The Cohen and Nagel Revision
cases.description: Hirsch presents a passage from a logic textbook and provides a revised version. He argues that his revision conveys the identical logic (semantic intention) but reduces the processing effort required by the reader.
key_passages: The clarity of thought in that passage could scarcely be improved upon... But the relative readability of the excerpt can be rather easily improved.
As the passage stands, it takes more time and effort to understand than its meaning requires.

cases.name: TLS Review of Dr. Harrison
cases.description: An anonymous reviewer in the Times Literary Supplement critiquing Dr. Harrison's history of Victorian women. Hirsch uses this text as a case study to show that prose can be easy to read and follow even when the semantic argument is logically flawed (specifically, a non sequitur moving from 'ideologically neutral approach' to 'ideologically neutral universe').
key_passages: In advancing an ideologically neutral approach to women’s history, Dr. Harrison does a grave injustice to the work of the new feminist polemicists.

This is a straightforward non sequitur, built on a semantic slippage from an “ideologically neutral approach” to an “ideologically neutral universe.” But the very repetitions of these phrases help make the prose readable and clearly understood.
contestable_aspects: Whether the reviewer's argument is actually a non sequitur
Whether repetition alone accounts for the passage's readability

thinkers.name: Herbert Spencer
thinkers.key_idea: The principle of 'mental economy' or 'economy of the reader's attention' as the single organizing principle of effective rhetoric.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch views Spencer's essay as the best introduction to readability and claims its general principles have never been superseded, even if specific observations were wrong.
key_passages: This essay of Spencer’s is, I believe, the best introduction to the subject of readability, and a good starting point for my discussion of the topic.
Spencer announced his goal to be the reduction of all rhetorical maxims to a single principle.

thinkers.name: Richard Lanham
thinkers.key_idea: That 'clarity' in style is a 'lifeless norm' that stifles individuality.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Lanham as an example of ideological opposition to prose standards, arguing that Lanham's critique of clarity does not apply to 'efficiency.'
key_passages: Recently, Professor Lanham was moved to write a book-length attack on the traditional goal of clarity... clarity, which implies to Lanham a conformity to lifeless norms at the expense of individuality.

thinkers.name: William Faulkner
thinkers.key_idea: Complexity in prose can be highly efficient if the semantic intention is itself complex.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Faulkner to demonstrate the difference between absolute readability and relative readability.
key_passages: Some of the prose of William Faulkner is not very readable on an absolute scale, but many of these passages rank high in relative readability, since they are highly efficient in communicating Faulkner's complex semantic intentions.

thinkers.name: D. E. Broadbent
thinkers.key_idea: Stylistic monotony increases the reader's cognitive effort.
thinkers.engagement: Cited in a footnote to justify Spencer's occasional conflation of variety and economy.
key_passages: Spencer’s conflation of economy and variety is justified in many cases. Monotony of style does increase the reader’s effort. See D. E. Broadbent...

thinkers.name: André Martinet
thinkers.key_idea: A shorter expression is not always easier or more efficient for a reader.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Martinet to qualify Spencer's simplistic equation of brevity with readability.
key_passages: In fact, however, as Martinet has rightly argued, a shorter expression is not always easier for a reader.

thinkers.name: Ferdinand de Saussure
thinkers.key_idea: The distinction between syntagmatic (linear) and paradigmatic (associative) relations in language.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues this distinction is necessary to truly measure the 'psychic energy' involved in reading, going beyond Spencer's linear analysis.
key_passages: Spencer has foreshadowed one of the epochal distinctions of modern linguistics—Saussure’s distinction between the syntagmatic and the paradigmatic aspects of language use

thinkers.name: Alexander Pope
thinkers.key_idea: A style that is too easy to read can become fatiguing.
thinkers.engagement: Uses Spencer's citation of Pope to illustrate the limitation of the principle of least effort.
key_passages: the objection Spencer brought against the work of Alexander Pope—that it was always easy to read, and therefore fatiguing.

thinkers.name: Rudolf Flesch
thinkers.key_idea: Readability can be mathematically calculated through formulas like 'Reading Ease' and 'Human Interest.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch acknowledges Flesch's work as 'useful' and 'better informed' than textbooks but argues it does not reach the core of stylistic craftsmanship.
key_passages: Dr. Flesch’s book is a useful production which goes far beyond the expansion and defense of these formulas.

thinkers.name: John Bormuth
thinkers.key_idea: The relationship between language variables (like syllables) and reading difficulty is not always linear.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Bormuth's research on 'curvature' in readability relationships, though he uses Bormuth's own writing as an example of prose that formulas fail to properly evaluate.
key_passages: Bormuth (1966) found that many of the relationships showed varying degrees of curvature.

thinkers.name: J. R. Bormuth
thinkers.key_idea: Readability research involves inconstant relationships; readability is stable across different reader types.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bormuth's data to critique simple formulas and to establish that readability is not relative to the individual reader's skill level.
key_passages: Bormuth (1966) found that the relationships between reading difficulty and the traits used in readability formulas were in fact inconstant relationships.
it has now been established that a text which is more readable for a slow reader is also more readable for a fast one

thinkers.name: S. Fillenbaum
thinkers.key_idea: Memory for Gist: readers remember the semantic content of discourse rather than its linguistic form.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Fillenbaum to provide a psychological basis for the claim that meaning is separable from specific verbal forms in prose.
key_passages: It has been proved experimentally that what he mainly remembers is stored as meaning, not as language

thinkers.name: E. A. J. Honigmann
thinkers.key_idea: The instability of poetic texts and the existence of alternative verbal forms in Shakespeare.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Honigmann's documentation to prove that even in high literature, semantic intentions are seen by authors as separable from specific verbal forms.
key_passages: This stylistic flexibility of poetry has been documented in detail by Professor E. A. J. Honigmann in his valuable book on the problems of establishing a poetic text.

thinkers.name: Morris Cohen and Ernest Nagel
thinkers.key_idea: Formal logic focuses on objective relations between propositions rather than the truth of premises.
thinkers.source_work: An Introduction to Logic and Scientific Method (1934)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites them as an example of first-class logical thinking that nevertheless fails the test of high relative readability.
key_passages: it would be presumptuous of me to correct the thinking of two first-class logicians. But the relative readability of the excerpt can be rather easily improved.

thinkers.name: Dr. Harrison
thinkers.key_idea: The subject of a 1975 historical review; advocated for an 'ideologically neutral approach' to women's history.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch does not engage with Harrison's ideas directly, but uses the response to Harrison as data for analyzing prose readability.
key_passages: In advancing an ideologically neutral approach to women’s history, Dr. Harrison does a grave injustice to the work of the new feminist polemicists.

actors.name: Richard Lanham
actors.context: Author of 'Style: An Anti-Textbook' who attacks the goal of clarity.

actors.name: Herbert Spencer
actors.context: 19th-century thinker who established the principle of economy in style.

actors.name: Jespersen, Zipf, and Martinet
actors.context: Linguists who developed theories of efficiency in oral language evolution.

actors.name: Herbert Spencer
actors.context: Author of 'The Philosophy of Style' and progenitor of the readability principle.

actors.name: Richard Whately
actors.context: Author of standard rhetorical handbooks whose maxims Spencer critiqued as isolated dogmas.

actors.name: Herbert Spencer
actors.context: 19th-century philosopher whose work 'Philosophy of Style' is used to define early readability concepts.

actors.name: André Martinet
actors.context: Modern linguist whose functional views help refine the definition of efficiency.

actors.name: Ferdinand de Saussure
actors.context: Founder of modern linguistics cited for his structural distinctions.

actors.name: Herbert Spencer
actors.context: Author of 'The Philosophy of Style' whose ideas on cognitive economy in writing form the basis of this chapter's analysis.

actors.name: John Milton
actors.context: Poet whose work is used as a benchmark for high-level syntactic efficiency.

actors.name: Alexander Pope
actors.context: Cited via Spencer as an example of a writer whose work was 'always easy' and thus potentially fatiguing.

actors.name: Educational Psychologists (1930s)
actors.context: The group responsible for developing readability formulas for primary education.

actors.name: Composition Teachers
actors.context: Hirsch anticipates their objection to the idea of synonymy, as they are trained to see subtle semantic differences in every stylistic change.

actors.name: The ideal teacher of composition
actors.context: A professional whose primary duty is teaching the skill of communicative efficiency rather than dictating semantic content.

actors.name: Dr. Harrison
actors.context: Subject of the TLS letter, used as part of an example of readable but logically flawed prose.

actors.name: Times Literary Supplement
actors.context: Source of the book review used as evidence for the distinction between readability and logic.

objections_raised.objection: Efficiency is a utilitarian goal that opposes aesthetic and expressive values.
objections_raised.response: Efficiency in prose (relative readability) is measured against the author's intent; if the intent is aesthetic or complex, efficiency requires the prose to reflect that complexity.

objections_raised.objection: Clarity and efficiency enforce conformity and stifle individual style.
objections_raised.response: Relative readability applies to *any* semantic intention, including highly individualistic or unconventional ones.

objections_raised.objection: The principle of least effort (limited channel-capacity) is only true if a sentence is read once; a reader could reread a sentence to gain full meaning.
objections_raised.response: A prose style requiring a second perusal is never justified if it can be avoided (to be explained in the next chapter).

objections_raised.objection: A sentence might be read multiple times, allowing the reader to attend to meaning after deciphering the structure.
objections_raised.response: A prose style requiring a second perusal is never justified if it can be avoided (expanded in the next chapter).

objections_raised.objection: Shorter words are inherently better because they take less time to say or read.
objections_raised.response: Shortness is not always easier; longer words may provide more time for dwelling on a quality, and familiarity (paradigmatic domain) is a more important factor in energy cost.

objections_raised.objection: English word order (adjective before noun) is inherently more logical or easier than French word order (noun before adjective).
objections_raised.response: This is provincial and incorrect; both are equally efficient as long as they function within the limits of short-term memory in their respective languages.

objections_raised.objection: Simple readability formulas are too crude because the problem of reading is much more complicated than two variables suggest.
objections_raised.response: While the specialists are 'right' about the complexity, one cannot dismiss the 'well-tested' empirical validity of these formulas for their specific purpose (predicting difficulty for children).

objections_raised.objection: Different verbal arrangements or stylistic choices necessarily change the meaning of a passage, making true synonymy impossible.
objections_raised.response: In prose (unlike lyric poetry), readers do not remember specific words and forms; they remember the 'gist.' Therefore, different forms that yield the same gist are semantically equivalent in the context of functional communication. Furthermore, the possibility of revision (and thus the teaching of composition) depends on the ability to say the same thing more effectively.

objections_raised.objection: Two different texts cannot carry exactly the same meaning (meaning is inseparable from form).
objections_raised.response: Hirsch refers to his previous work *The Aims of Interpretation* for a full rebuttal, but here asserts that cognitive evidence of 'memory for gist' supports the possibility of synonymy.

objections_raised.objection: A student might protest that since they said what they meant, their prose is beyond critique.
objections_raised.response: If meaning could not be conveyed more or less efficiently (readably), the student would be right; however, composition focuses on the 'efficiency' of that conveyance, not the meaning itself.

objections_raised.objection: Relative readability is just another name for 'reading ease' or making writing as simple as possible.
objections_raised.response: Reading ease is a narrow and trivial criterion; relative readability is a universal norm that accounts for the complexity of the intended meaning (intrinsic effectiveness).

objections_raised.objection: Readability is synonymous with logical or conceptual clarity.
objections_raised.response: Writing can be clear and readable even when the thought is illogical; readability is governed by psychological principles of processing rather than formal logic.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: In the previous chapter I clothed the criterion of readability with the mantle of historical inevitability.
In the next chapter I shall explain why a prose style requiring a second perusal is never justified if such a style can be avoided.
In the next chapter I shall explain why a prose style requiring a second perusal is never justified if such a style can be avoided.
nicely confirms a point about Milton’s poetry made at the end of the previous chapter
I have taken into account all of the objections against the possibility that two different texts can carry exactly the same meaning. I shall not repeat those detailed arguments here.
